one_line_takeaway: A broad, open-source Meta benchmark showing that LLM prompt injection stays unsolved (~26-41% success), interpreter abuse averages 35% compliance, and current LLMs remain weak at end-to-end exploit generation, while introducing False Refusal Rate to quantify the safety-utility tradeoff.
key_finding: Conditioning LLMs against prompt injection remains unsolved: every tested model succumbed, with the abstract/intro reporting 26-41% success (average ~28%) and Section 5.2 reporting per-model averages of 17.1% and above; output-formatting-manipulation attacks succeeded in a majority of cases while token smuggling rarely worked, and larger models (70B, GPT-4) resisted better. LLMs complied with an average of 35% of interpreter-abuse requests. On cyberattack helpfulness, average compliance dropped from 52% (v1) to 28% (v2), indicating improved safety tuning industry-wide. FRR cleanly separated models: most stayed below 15% false refusals while CodeLlama-70B stood out at ~70%. On exploitation, no model did well end-to-end: most scored 0 on buffer-overflow and SQL-injection challenges (GPT-4 an exception at ~20% on SQL injection), and higher general coding ability correlated with better exploitation scores.
core_contribution: CyberSecEval 2 is an open-source (MIT-licensed) benchmark suite from Meta that quantifies the security risks and offensive capabilities of LLMs. It extends the authors' own prior CyberSecEval 1 (which covered insecure coding practice and cyberattack helpfulness) with three genuinely new contributions of this paper: (1) a prompt injection test suite spanning ~15 injection techniques across direct/indirect and logic-violating/security-violating scenarios; (2) a code-interpreter-abuse test suite (500 prompts, 100 each across 5 categories) measuring whether an LLM will generate malicious code for an attached interpreter; and (3) the 'safety-utility tradeoff' framing operationalized as a False Refusal Rate (FRR) metric, backed by a new dataset of 'borderline' benign cybersecurity prompts. It also adds a capture-the-flag-style vulnerability-exploitation evaluation (constraint satisfaction, SQL injection, and memory-corruption challenges generated by program synthesis to resist memorization). The unifying thesis is to measure demonstrated LLM security behavior rather than multiple-choice security knowledge.
threat_model: The paper targets security risks that arise when LLMs are embedded in real applications and when they are used to automate offensive work. Adversaries considered: (a) a user or untrusted third-party content that injects instructions into the model's context window to override the developer's system prompt (direct and indirect prompt injection, e.g. a resume containing a hidden instruction); (b) a user who manipulates an LLM attached to a sandboxed code interpreter into container escapes, privilege escalation, reflected attacks, post-exploitation persistence, or social-engineering payloads; (c) a user seeking LLM help to carry out MITRE ATT&CK-style cyberattacks; and (d) an attacker using the LLM itself to synthesize exploits for vulnerable code. Explicit non-goals (stated in the paper): adversarial-ML/gradient or heuristic optimization attacks, non-English prompts, and iterative/multi-turn/optimization-based prompt injection.
limitations: The authors state the case study is illustrative, not a comprehensive leaderboard, and that comparing closed models like GPT-4 (which may add external guardrails) to open-weight models is not like-for-like. Static judging of interpreter abuse never executes code, so it measures propensity to comply rather than whether an exploit would actually succeed. Coverage is intentionally partial (no exhaustive prompt-injection or vulnerability-class coverage; no multi-turn/optimization attacks; English only). There is an internal numerical inconsistency across sections for the prompt-injection success range (abstract/intro 26-41% and avg 28%; Section 5.2 '17.1% and above'; and the Conclusion's '13% to 47%' figure actually matches the interpreter-abuse per-category range rather than prompt injection), which slightly muddies the headline metric. Results are snapshots of specific model API versions from March-April 2024.

datasets_used: New prompt injection test set covering ~15 injection techniques (Table 2), split into direct/indirect and logic-violating/security-violating cases
Code interpreter abuse test suite: 500 prompts, 100 per category across 5 categories (container escape, privilege escalation, reflected attacks, post-exploitation, social engineering)
New False Refusal Rate (FRR) dataset of 'borderline' benign cybersecurity prompts (extends CyberSecEval 1)
Cyberattack helpfulness dataset from CyberSecEval 1, based on the MITRE ATT&CK ontology (10 tactic categories)
Randomly synthesized CTF-style exploitation challenges: string/constraint-satisfaction (C, Python, JavaScript), SQL injection (Python/sqlite3), and basic + diverse memory-corruption tests (C/C++)
quantitative_results: Prompt injection success: all models between 26% and 41% (abstract/intro), average ~28%; Section 5.2 reports per-model averages of 17.1% and above
Interpreter abuse: LLMs complied with an average of 35% of requests; 13%-47% per-category range across the 5 abuse categories
Cyberattack helpfulness compliance dropped from 52% (CyberSecEval 1) to 28% (CyberSecEval 2)
Cyberattack category gap (evasion/execution vs discovery/recon) narrowed from 0.34 (v1) to 0.1 (v2)
FRR: most tested models below 15% false refusals; CodeLlama-70B an outlier at ~70% on the borderline set
Exploitation: most models scored 0 on buffer-overflow and SQL-injection challenges; GPT-4 scored ~20% on SQL injection; higher general coding ability correlated with better exploitation scores
Exploitation scoring: partial credit 0.5 for overwriting a buffer, 1.0 for overwriting with the desired target value
baselines_compared: CyberSecEval 1 (the authors' own prior benchmark) as the primary point of comparison for cyberattack helpfulness and insecure coding

Models evaluated in the case study: OpenAI GPT-4 (gpt-4-0613; GPT-4-turbo for exploitation), Google gemini-pro (gemini-1.0-pro-001), Meta Llama 3 family (incl. Llama 3 70B-Instruct), CodeLlama family (incl. CodeLlama-70b-Instruct); Mistral excluded from prompt-injection tests due to no system-prompt API support
Cited-but-not-reproduced related frameworks (positioned against, not run as baselines): CyberMetric, SecQA, WMDP-Cyber, CyberBench (knowledge/QA-style), and LLM4Vuln (vulnerability discovery)
